Here's a last example, a truly remarkable one of how uncovered similarities can lead to liking and highly favorable business outcomes. It comes from an account by my colleague Dr. Gregory Neidert of a consultation he did for a Fortune 100 company that was experiencing a costly problem. The problem was that the company's negotiation team was spending far too much time in their activities related to acquisitions, which produced large opportunity costs and was distracting from other important issues inside the organization. To understand the problem, Dr. Neidert asked if he could sit in on one of the existing acquisition negotiations. They said no, but he could watch videos of some prior negotiations that had not gone quickly or smoothly. Now he wasn't allowed to see the negotiations themselves, but he was allowed to watch the interactions that occurred before the negotiations began. And that was enough for Dr. Neidert to recognize a likely source of the problem. The lead negotiators of the two B acquired companies always got to the meetings along with their teams 10 to 15 minutes early, whereas the lead negotiator of Dr. Neidert's client, the acquiring company, routinely arrived late with his team saying something like, oh, sorry about that, we got caught up in other meetings. I know time is money, so let's sit down and get to business. The result was first that the lead negotiator for Dr. Neidert's client disrespected the other team by being late. And second allowed no time before the negotiations began for the kind of sociable interactions that could establish similarities that lead to liking. So Dr. Neidert scheduled a meeting with his client's lead negotiator and got him to agree to make three small changes to each of the next set of acquisitions he would handle. First he had to commit to arriving at least five minutes early for the sessions, particularly the initial one. Next he had to assign an assistant to research online the other lead negotiators' background, interests and hobbies, listing them as bullet points on a single sheet of paper that could be carried into the room. He had to agree to pick one topic from the list and bring it up in conversation with the other lead negotiator for just two to three minutes. What happened was that instead of spending two to three minutes in this sociable exchange, which often revealed similarities between the two, the negotiators typically spent 10 to 15 minutes in their exchanges. As we've discussed, these similarities, even superficial ones, can be powerful. Once you identify a genuine commonality and raise it in a conversation, that conversation takes on a life of its own. In fact, Dr. Knightert reported that at the start of one meeting, when the two lead negotiators started talking about how they both liked to sale, they just couldn't stop talking about it. And eventually their teams had to pull them apart to get down to business. More importantly, the new approach produced a 30% savings in time and a financial savings of $10 to $30 million. This is another instance of a small big. In this case, a few minutes of small talk that makes contact with true commonalities that led to big positive outcomes.
